title: 26 Nehase — St. Joshua son of Nun & The Assembly
saints: St. Joshua son of Nun (Monthly Commemoration)
narrative:
On the twenty-sixth day of Nehase, the Church honors St. Joshua son of Nun in his monthly commemoration — and the Ethiopian calendar's inclusion of Joshua among the monthly saints speaks to the Tewahedo tradition's deep rootedness in the entire sweep of scriptural history, from Adam at the beginning of the month to the prophets and judges and kings who appear throughout. Joshua is the man who completed what Moses began: he led the people across the Jordan and into the Promised Land, distributing the inheritance that God had sworn to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. His name in Hebrew — Yehoshua — is the same name that the New Testament renders as Jesus, and the Fathers of the Church universally read Joshua as a type of Christ: the one who brings the people through the waters of death (the Jordan = baptism) and into the inheritance of eternal life (Canaan = the Kingdom of God).

The resonance between Joshua and the conclusion of Nehase is profound. The month has been a journey — from Adam's fall, through the Transfiguration's light, through fifteen days of fast, through the Assumption's glory, through the feast of Ethiopia's greatest saint — and now, on the twenty-sixth day, the figure of the conqueror-inheritor appears to name what this whole journey has been about. The faithful have been crossing a Jordan of their own: the disciplines of the month, the fast of Filseta, the commemorations of martyrs and confessors and prophets, have been a progressive crossing into a wider and more spacious life in God. Joshua stands at the bank and says: cross over; the inheritance is real; the promises of God do not fail.

Sts. Adrian and Natalia are one of the Church's most beloved married-couple martyrdoms. Adrian was a pagan official in Nicomedia who was so struck by the endurance and joy of the Christian prisoners he was guarding that he declared himself a Christian and was imprisoned with them. His wife Natalia, secretly already a Christian, cared for him and the other prisoners during their confinement, and urged him not to recant. He was executed; she survived him and died peacefully, her witness that of the faithful spouse who accompanied her husband to the very threshold of death and saw him through to the other side. Their story is an icon of married holiness: the covenant of husband and wife becomes, in the crucible of persecution, a covenant of mutual martyrdom.

St. Ibestion the Martyr adds his voice to the universal chorus of those who sealed their faith with blood, and together with Adrian and Natalia stands as testimony that no station in life — military official, devoted wife, anonymous believer — exempts the Christian from the call to total witness. As Nehase approaches its final days, Joshua, Adrian, Natalia, and Ibestion together compose a final image of the promised life: the inheritance is real, the crossing is possible, the cost is known, and those who have paid it are not diminished but glorified.
